pattern: Because a larger candidate maximum difference can only make it easier to assemble p disjoint pairs, binary searching on the candidate answer and checking feasibility with a greedy adjacent-pairing sweep over the sorted values pins down the true minimum.
hints: First ask: for one fixed candidate maximum difference d, what is the fastest way to check whether p disjoint pairs, each within d of each other, can be formed at all?
Sort the values, then sweep left to right: whenever two neighbors differ by at most d, commit them as a pair immediately and skip past both -- this never wastes an element compared to waiting.

The number of pairs this greedy sweep can find only grows as d grows, so binary search for the smallest d whose greedy count reaches p -- and remember p = 0 always answers 0, since the max of an empty set is defined as zero.

edge_inputs.description: p = 0 must answer 0 regardless of how spread out the values are
smallest possible array, p at its maximum of n/2
duplicate values let every pair achieve a difference of zero
p equal to n/2, every element must be paired
a single far outlier forces a large difference when only one pair is possible
already-sorted ascending values where the best pairing is not simply the first two elements
every value identical, so any pairing of any size costs zero

stress.description: random arrays with a random feasible p, exercising many different sorted-gap structures
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 4000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 10**9) for _ in range(n)]
    p = rng.randint(0, n // 2)
    return [nums, p]
